title: NOC 92101 – Water and waste treatment plant operators | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 92101 professionals are the guardians of public health and environmental sustainability. As a TEER 2 occupation, these operators manage the sophisticated computerized systems that purify drinking water and treat wastewater before it returns to the ecosystem. In 2026, as Canada faces increased urbanization and stricter environmental regulations, the demand for "Smart Water" technicians who can operate AI-integrated filtration systems is at an all-time high. For immigration, this role is a top-tier candidate for Express Entry Category-Based Selection and the New 2026 TR to PR Pathway.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Level2Major Group92 – Processing, manufacturing and utilities supervisorsSub-Major Group921 – Utilities equipment operators and controllersMinor Group9210 – Utilities equipment operators and controllersNOC Code92101

duties: System Operation: Monitor and operate computerized control systems to regulate the treatment and distribution of water or the disposal of sewage.
Monitoring & Inspection: Inspect plant equipment and systems (pumps, valves, filters) to detect malfunctions and ensure normal operation.
Testing & Analysis: Collect and test water/waste samples for chemical and bacterial content; analyze results and adjust equipment to ensure quality control.
Compliance: Ensure that all treated water meets standardized federal and provincial quality requirements (e.g., chlorine and fluoride levels).
Maintenance: Perform preventive maintenance or assist skilled tradespersons with the installation and repair of plant machinery.
Safety & Security: Perform security checks on grounds and respond to public enquiries regarding water quality issues or emergencies.
Reporting: Complete and maintain detailed plant logs, reports, and statistics for regulatory audits.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is mandatory.
Education: A college diploma or vocational program in water/wastewater technology, environmental science, or a related field is often required.

Certifications (Mandatory):Drinking Water Certification is required in Ontario and Quebec for treatment and distribution.Wastewater Treatment Certification (Level I to IV, depending on the plant classification) is mandatory across most provinces.WHMIS (Workplace Hazardous Materials Information System) and TDG (Transportation of Dangerous Goods) certificates.
Drinking Water Certification is required in Ontario and Quebec for treatment and distribution.
Wastewater Treatment Certification (Level I to IV, depending on the plant classification) is mandatory across most provinces.
WHMIS (Workplace Hazardous Materials Information System) and TDG (Transportation of Dangerous Goods) certificates.
Physical ability for equipment checks and a high level of analytical reasoning are essential.
salary_prospects: Note: Chief Operators and Team Leads in 2026 can earn annual salaries exceeding $105,000, especially within large municipal systems or industrial facilities.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. Projections indicate that the utilities sector will see a steady wave of retirements over the next three years. As the federal government invests billions into "Climate-Resilient Infrastructure," the demand for operators capable of managing high-capacity, storm-hardened treatment facilities will continue to grow through the end of the decade.
labour_market_demand: The demand for water and waste treatment operators is Good to Very Good across Canada. In 2026, Ontario, British Columbia, and Alberta are reporting steady job growth due to aging infrastructure replacement and new municipal developments. According to 2026 Job Bank outlooks, the sector is experiencing a "Greening of the Workforce," where there is a specific shortage of operators certified in advanced nutrient removal and membrane filtration technologies.
Environmental Impact: Play a direct role in Canada’s "Blue Economy" by protecting the world's largest freshwater supply.
Technological Advancement: Gain experience with SCADA systems, automated chemical dosing, and real-time sensor-based water quality monitoring.
Recession-Proof Career: As an essential public utility, this sector offers exceptional job security with 76% of workers employed year-round.
Strategic PR Pathways: This role is a high priority for the Express Entry category-based draws for "Trade Occupations" and the Rural Community Immigration Pilot (RCIP).
pathways: Express Entry (Category-Based Selection): High priority for candidates with experience in "Trade Occupations."
New 2026 TR to PR Pathway: A temporary measure launching in April 2026 targeting skilled workers already in Canada, with a focus on essential service sectors.
OINP Employer Job Offer (In-Demand Skills): Frequent draws for Water Treatment Operators to support Ontario’s growing municipalities.
Rural Community Immigration Pilot (RCIP): A direct pathway for operators willing to work in rural communities facing critical labor shortages.
